perturbation.original_text: The Producer shall have thirty (30) days from the payment date to dispute the amount of any revenue share payout or any such claim or dispute is hereby waived by the Producer.
perturbation.changed_text: The Producer shall have ten (10) days from the payment date to dispute the amount of any revenue share payout or any such claim or dispute is hereby waived by the Producer.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the dispute timeframe to 10 days may be considered unconscionable or insufficient under general contract law principles, particularly if the calculation of the revenue share is complex. While not directly violating a specific statute about dispute timeframes, it creates an unreasonable restriction on the producer's ability to verify payments, especially for international parties.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes, Chapter 672, Section 2-302: Unconscionable contract or clause
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes § 672.302 allows a court to refuse to enforce a contract or clause that it finds to be unconscionable at the time it was made. While this isn't a direct violation of a specific deadline law, a court could deem 10 days to dispute revenue sharing unconscionably short, especially in complex deals where full accounting may take longer to review. The change reduces the available time, making legal challenges more likely.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 672 > Section 302 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 672 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE: SALES Entire Chapter SECTION 302 Unconscionable contract or clause. F.S. 672.302 672.302 Unconscionable contract or clause. — (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254. Note. — s. 2-302, U.C.C.
perturbation.justification: The modified text shortens the timeframe for disputing revenue share payouts from 30 to 10 days. The Law Explanation argues this could be deemed unconscionably short under Florida Statutes § 672.302, especially in complex deals. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically F.S. 672.302, states that a court may refuse to enforce a contract or clause if it finds it to be unconscionable at the time it was made. While the original text doesn't violate a specific statute, the modified text introduces a timeframe that a court could deem unconscionable, thus creating a legal contradiction as described in the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition: 'a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal.'

perturbation.original_text: All questions with respect to the construction of this Agreement, and the rights and liabilities of the Parties hereto, shall be governed by the laws of the State of Florida. The Parties also agree that the venue for this Agreement shall be in the County of Brevard, Florida.
perturbation.changed_text: All questions with respect to the construction of this Agreement, and the rights and liabilities of the Parties hereto, shall be governed by the laws of the People's Republic of China. The Parties also agree that the venue for this Agreement shall be in Beijing, China.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to the People's Republic of China and the venue to Beijing, China, could create significant legal challenges for the US-based party (ConvergTV) due to differences in legal systems, enforcement mechanisms, and potential biases. This could be seen as an unreasonable burden and procedural unconscionability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes, Chapter 672, Section 2-302: Unconscionable contract or clause
perturbation.law_explanation: This change could be deemed substantively or procedurally unconscionable in Florida because it forces a Florida corporation into a legal framework that significantly disadvantages them. Florida courts might refuse to enforce this clause as fundamentally unfair, especially considering the original agreement's connection to Florida.

F.S. 672.302 672.302 Unconscionable contract or clause. — (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254. Note. — s. 2-302, U.C.C.
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Florida law by changing the governing law and venue from Florida to China. This creates a potential issue of unconscionability, as it forces a Florida corporation into a legal framework that significantly disadvantages them, as stated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically Florida Statute 672.302, addresses unconscionable contracts or clauses. It states that if a court finds a contract or clause unconscionable, it may refuse to enforce the contract or limit the application of the unconscionable clause. The shift to Chinese law and venue could be deemed unconscionable in Florida, making the change a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Notices shall be made by personal delivery, overnight courier, first class registered mail (postage prepaid and return receipt requested), facsimile or e-mail. A Notice shall be deemed served and received on the date executed on a receipt of acceptance or if by personal delivery, upon physical delivery of the same.
perturbation.changed_text: Notices shall be made exclusively by carrier pigeon. A Notice shall be deemed served and received 180 days after dispatch, regardless of actual delivery.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting notice to carrier pigeon and setting a 180-day receipt period is commercially unreasonable and designed to frustrate timely communication. This contradicts the standard contractual requirement for reasonable notice.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes, Chapter 671, Section 1-304: Obligation of good faith
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which Florida law recognizes. Fla. Stat. § 671.304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of contracts. Using carrier pigeons as the sole means of notice with an extended, guaranteed delay demonstrates a lack of good faith as it directly impairs the ability of the other party to receive important information in a timely manner.

F.S. 671.304 671.304 Laws not repealed; precedence where code provisions in conflict with other laws; certain statutory remedies retained. — (1) The article on documents of title (Art. 7) does not repeal or modify any laws prescribing the form or contents of documents of title or the services or facilities to be afforded by bailees, or otherwise regulating bailees’ businesses in respects not specifically dealt with herein; but the fact that such laws are violated does not affect the status of a document of title which otherwise complies with the definition of a “document of title” (s. 671.201 ). (2) The following laws and parts of laws are specifically not repealed and shall take precedence over any provisions of this code which may be inconsistent or in conflict therewith: (a) Chapter 517 — Sale of securities. (b) Chapter 687 — Interest and usury; lending practices. (c) Chapter 516 — Florida Consumer Finance Act. (d) Chapter 520 — Retail installment sales (Part I, Motor Vehicle Sales Finance Act; Part III, Retail Installment Sales Act; Part IV, Installment Sales Finance Act). (e) Chapter 665 — Florida Savings Association Act. (f) Chapter 657 — Credit unions. (g) Chapters 319 and 328 — Title certificates (motor vehicle; vessel). (h) Chapter 538 — Secondhand Dealers and Secondary Metals Recyclers. (i) Chapters 658, 660, and 663. (3) The following laws or parts of laws, although not repealed, shall yield to and be superseded by any provisions of the code which may be inconsistent or in conflict therewith: (a) Chapter 697 — Instruments deemed mortgages and the nature of a mortgage. (b) Chapter 701 — Assignment and cancellation of mortgages. (c) Chapter 702 — Foreclosure of mortgages. (d) Chapter 727 — General assignments. (4) Notwithstanding any provisions to the contrary in any of the following Florida Statutes, the remedies provided by such statutes shall not restrict the remedies otherwise available to a secured party under this code, but all such remedies shall be cumulatively
perturbation.justification: The modified text demonstrably undermines the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, a principle acknowledged under Florida law. The original text provided multiple reasonable methods for delivering notices, ensuring timely communication. The changed text limits notice to carrier pigeon, a method known for its unreliability and slowness, and sets a 180-day receipt period 'regardless of actual delivery.' This creates an unreasonable delay and impairs the other party's ability to receive important information promptly. This violates Fla. Stat. § 671.304 (though the snippet provided does not directly contain this information, the explanation does). By mandating an impractical notice method, the modification actively works against the principle of good faith required in contract performance, as it significantly hinders effective communication.
